question_text: Verify the address of the emergency. What is the exact address?
What is the callback number you are calling from?
Tell me exactly what happened.
How old is the patient?
Is s/he breathing?
Is s/he breathing normally, or is it gasping, gurgling, or irregular?
Can you feel a pulse? Check the side of the neck.
Tap the shoulders firmly and shout — does s/he respond at all?
Does s/he respond to pain? Pinch the earlobe — any reaction?
Are the eyes open? Do they respond to your voice?
When was s/he last seen normal and awake?
Did anyone witness what happened?
Did s/he collapse suddenly or gradually become unresponsive?
Was there any seizure activity — shaking or jerking?
Is there any bleeding or sign of trauma?
What position is s/he in? Is s/he on the floor, in a chair, in bed?
Are there any pill bottles, syringes, or drugs nearby?
Is there any vomit? Is the airway clear?
What color is the skin — normal, pale, blue, or flushed?
Is the skin warm, cold, or sweaty?
Does s/he have any medical conditions — diabetes, epilepsy, heart disease?
Does s/he wear a medical alert bracelet or necklace?
Is s/he diabetic? Is there a glucometer available?
Has s/he been ill recently?
Could s/he have been exposed to carbon monoxide, fumes, or chemicals?
Is anyone else in the building feeling ill?
ECHO-level: Not breathing and no pulse — cardiac arrest
ECHO-level: Agonal breathing only (gasping/gurgling) — treat as cardiac arrest
DELTA-level: Unconscious, not breathing but has a pulse — respiratory arrest
DELTA-level: Unconscious with signs of major trauma — bleeding, deformity
DELTA-level: Unconscious with suspected overdose — pinpoint pupils, syringes nearby
DELTA-level: Unconscious after sudden collapse — suspected cardiac event
CHARLIE-level: Unconscious but breathing normally, responds to pain
condition: unconscious AND breathing AND pain response

question_text: CHARLIE-level: Unconscious diabetic — possible hypoglycemia
CHARLIE-level: Postictal state after witnessed seizure, breathing
BRAVO-level: Altered consciousness — responds to voice, confused, not fully alert
BRAVO-level: Fainting episode, now regaining consciousness, oriented
ALPHA-level: Brief fainting, now fully alert, oriented, no injury
condition: brief syncope AND fully alert AND no injury

question_text: ALPHA-level: Near-syncope, did not fully lose consciousness, stable
OMEGA-level: Vasovagal episode with clear trigger, fully recovered, no cardiac history
condition: vasovagal AND recovered AND no cardiac history

question_text: If not breathing and no pulse: begin CPR immediately. Place the heel of your hand on the center of the chest. Push hard and fast — at least 2 inches deep, 100-120 pushes per minute.
After 30 compressions, tilt the head back, lift the chin, and give 2 breaths. Watch for the chest to rise.
If an AED is available, turn it on and follow the voice prompts. Do not stop CPR until the AED tells you to.
If breathing but unconscious: roll the patient onto their side in the recovery position to protect the airway.
If there is vomit in the mouth, turn the head to the side and clear it with your finger.
If you suspect an opioid overdose and have naloxone (Narcan), administer it now.
Do NOT give an unconscious patient anything by mouth.
Keep the patient warm with a blanket.
Stay on the line. Help is on the way. Keep doing CPR if instructed — do not stop until paramedics arrive.
If anything changes, tell me immediately.
Do not hang up until I tell you to.
